cardImage
Trekkr
Hello blank fragment
An iron lattice tower built in 1889, standing 330m tall. Originally criticised, it is now one of the most visited monuments in the world.
An oval amphitheatre built in 70–80 AD, capable of holding up to 80,000 spectators for gladiatorial contests and public spectacles.
A 15th-century Inca citadel set high in the Andes Mountains at 2,430m. Rediscovered in 1911 by Hiram Bingham.
A series of fortifications stretching over 21,000 km, built across centuries to protect Chinese states from invasions.
A white marble mausoleum built by Mughal emperor Shah Jahan in 1643 in memory of his beloved wife Mumtaz Mahal.
A colossal neoclassical sculpture gifted by France to the USA in 1886. Standing 93m tall, she holds a torch and a tablet inscribed with July 4, 1776.
A multi-venue performing arts centre opened in 1973. Its distinctive sail-shaped shells have made it one of the most recognisable buildings in the world.
An ancient city carved into rose-red sandstone cliffs, once the thriving capital of the Nabataean Kingdom around 300 BC.
A large pre-Columbian city built by the Maya. The 30m pyramid El Castillo served as a temple to the god Kukulkan.
A volcanic island famous for its dramatic cliffs, whitewashed buildings with blue domes, and spectacular sunsets over the caldera.
The largest religious monument in the world, built in the 12th century as a Hindu temple and later converted to Buddhism.
A rocky hilltop citadel containing the remains of ancient buildings, most notably the Parthenon, built in 447–438 BC.
A 38m tall Art Deco statue of Jesus Christ, inaugurated in 1931 atop Corcovado mountain overlooking Rio de Janeiro.
A prehistoric stone circle erected around 2500 BC. Its exact purpose remains debated — astronomical calendar, burial site, or religious monument.
Japan\'s highest peak at 3,776m, an active stratovolcano last erupting in 1707. A sacred site and iconic symbol of Japan.
One of the world\'s largest waterfalls, 1.7 km wide and 108m tall. Local name \'Mosi-oa-Tunya\' means \'The Smoke That Thunders\'.
The world\'s tallest building at 828m, completed in 2010. Its design was inspired by the Hymenocallis desert flower.
A large unfinished Roman Catholic church designed by Antoni Gaudí, under construction since 1882 and still ongoing today.
The Aurora Borealis — a natural light display caused by charged solar particles colliding with Earth\'s atmosphere. Best seen September to March.
A volcanic archipelago in the Pacific famous for its unique wildlife, including giant tortoises and marine iguanas. Darwin visited in 1835.
A steep-sided canyon carved by the Colorado River, 446 km long and up to 1,800m deep, exposing nearly 2 billion years of Earth\'s history.
The world\'s largest tropical rainforest, covering 5.5 million km². Home to 10% of all species on Earth and often called \'the lungs of the planet\'.
Originally a cathedral built in 537 AD, later an Ottoman mosque, then a museum, and reclassified as a mosque again in 2020.
A vast ecosystem of 30,000 km² famous for the annual Great Migration, where over 1.5 million wildebeest cross the plains.
A system of 275 individual waterfalls on the border of Argentina and Brazil. Eleanor Roosevelt reportedly said: \'Poor Niagara!\'
